+ Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 81.3
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The saxophone hit me first. Not the heat, not the smell of frying shrimp and wet asphalt, not the crush of bodies on a sidewalk that felt like it was vibrating. The sax. A low, mournful wail that curled up from somewhere ahead, weaving through the noise of car horns and laughter like a living thing. It matched the feeling in my chest—a hollow, aching sound that had no home. *Okay, Taylor. You did it. You’re here. Now what?* The question sat in my gut like a stone. Two states away. No phone—I’d ditched it in a gas station trash can outside of Jackson because Mom had already texted seventeen times and the last one just said *Please.* I couldn’t read *Please* again. Not after the look on her face when I told her. Not after Dad’s silence, which was worse than anything he could’ve yelled. So now I was standing on Decatur Street, duffel bag slung over one shoulder, wearing a hoodie that was already too hot for Louisiana in September. People flowed around me like I was a rock in a river. A woman in sequins bumped my elbow and didn’t apologize. A guy selling beaded necklaces gave me a once-over and looked away. *You’re invisible. Good. That’s good. That’s what you wanted.* But it wasn’t. I wanted to be seen. Just not by *them.* I started walking toward the sax. The sound pulled me past a bar with wrought-iron balconies and a guy vomiting into a trash can at eleven in the morning. Past a tarot card reader who called out, “You’re lost, honey,” which was either a hell of a guess or the most obvious statement in the world. The music got louder, and then I saw him. He was maybe a year or two older than me, perched on a crate in front of a shuttered storefront. His saxophone was old, brass dented and tarnished, but the sound coming out of it was pure gold. His eyes were closed, and he swayed like the music was the only thing holding him upright. A beat-up guitar case lay open at his feet, scattered with a few crumpled bills and some coins. I stopped. I didn’t mean to, but my feet just… stopped. The song was sad and beautiful and it felt like it was about me, even though it couldn’t be. It felt like it was about running, and about the moment you realize you can’t run far enough. He finished with a long, breathy note that hung in the air for a second before dissolving into the street noise. His eyes opened. They were a strange shade of gray-green, and they landed on me immediately. “You look like you just lost your dog,” he said. His voice was rough, like he didn’t use it much. “I don’t have a dog,” I said. “Then you lost something else.” He set the sax across his knees and studied me. “Where you headed?” *Home. Away. Anywhere but here.* “I don’t know yet.” He nodded like that made perfect sense. “First time in NOLA?” “First time out of Tennessee, actually.” I winced. That was too much information. Mom always said I overshared when I was nervous. But he didn’t look bored or judgmental. He just tilted his head. “You got a place to crash?” “Working on it.” “Right.” He reached into his pocket and pulled out a crumpled twenty. “Here. Get some food. You look like you’re about to fall over.” I stared at the bill. “I can’t take your money.” “It’s not my money. It’s theirs.” He gestured at the guitar case. “I’m just redistributing. Take it.” I took it. My hand was shaking. I hated that my hand was shaking. “Thanks. I’m Taylor.” “Remy.” He stood up, and I saw that he was taller than me, lanky, with a tattoo peeking out from his collar—a bird in flight, maybe a swallow. “You play?” He nodded at my duffel. “No. I mean, I don’t have an instrument.” “That’s not what I asked.” He smiled, and it was a tired smile, but it was real. “You play?” I didn’t know what he meant. But something in his voice made me feel like he was asking a different question. *Are you running? Are you hiding? Are you scared?* “I don’t know,” I said. “Maybe. I used to draw.” “Bring your sketchbook?” “Left it behind.” He nodded again, like that was also expected. “Well. You gotta do something, or you’ll go crazy. That’s the rule.” He picked up his sax and slung the strap over his shoulder. “I’m gonna play another set. You can stick around if you want. Or don’t. Your call.” I should’ve walked away. I knew nothing about this guy. He could be dangerous. He could be anything. Mom’s voice was in my head—*stranger danger, Taylor, you don’t know what people are capable of*—but Mom’s voice was also the voice that had said *maybe this is a phase* and *can’t you just wait until you’re eighteen?* So I sat down on the curb, a few feet from his crate, and I listened. He played for an hour. People came and went. Some dropped coins. Some just walked past. I watched the crowd and tried to figure out who was a threat and who wasn’t, but everyone looked like they were playing a part. The businesswoman with the tight bun who smiled at her phone. The old man with the cane who stopped to tap his foot. The kid, maybe twelve, who hovered at the edge of the crowd, watching Remy with hungry eyes. When Remy finally stopped, he was sweating. He wiped his forehead with his sleeve and looked at me. “Still here.” “Still here.” “You hungry?” “Starving.” He packed up his sax and we walked to a Po’ Boy shop two blocks over. He ordered for both of us without asking what I wanted, and I didn’t care. The sandwich was greasy and perfect and I ate it too fast and almost choked. “So,” he said, picking at his own bread. “What’s your deal?” “What’s yours?” “Fair.” He chewed. “I’ve been on the street for about six months. Ran away from a group home in Baton Rouge. Don’t ask me why. I won’t tell you the whole story, but the short version is: I don’t trust adults, and I don’t trust cops, and I sleep in a warehouse with three other kids who have the same policy.” I put my sandwich down. “Why are you telling me this?” “Because you’re gonna find out anyway, and I’d rather you hear it from me.” He met my eyes. “You can crash with us tonight if you want. But you should know: I’m not doing it to be nice. I’m doing it because I see something in your face that I recognize. And that scares me a little.” My throat tightened. “What do you see?” “Someone who’s used to being disappointed.” He said it flatly, like a fact. “And someone who’s about to make a really bad decision if they don’t get a break.” I didn’t know if I could trust him. I didn’t know if I could trust anyone. But the sun was going down, and the saxophone was still echoing in my head, and the only other option was to keep walking into the dark. “Okay,” I said. “Show me.”
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional authenticity in depicting a transgender teen's first day as a runaway in New Orleans. The writing successfully captures Taylor's vulnerable yet resilient voice through effective use of internal monologue and sensory details. The opening is particularly strong, using the saxophone as both literal scene-setting and metaphorical representation of Taylor's emotional state. The author skillfully weaves together external observations with internal conflict, creating a believable portrait of someone processing trauma while navigating an unfamiliar environment. Character development is nuanced throughout. Taylor feels authentic as a 16-year-old - oversharing when nervous, simultaneously craving visibility and invisibility, making impulsive decisions driven by desperation. Remy emerges as a complex figure who avoids typical "wise street mentor" clichés through his matter-of-fact delivery and acknowledgment of his own limitations. The dialogue feels natural and age-appropriate, particularly Remy's blunt honesty about his motivations and Taylor's defensive responses. The conversation flows organically while revealing character depth and advancing the plot. The prose maintains consistent quality without becoming overwrought. Descriptions serve the narrative effectively - the sequined woman who doesn't apologize, the vomiting man, the tarot reader's obvious observation all contribute to the atmosphere without feeling forced. The pacing works well, moving from disorientation through tentative connection to a realistic decision point. The ending avoids false optimism while providing enough hope to feel earned given the circumstances. Minor weaknesses include some predictable elements (the street musician with wisdom, the offer of shelter) and occasional moments where the internal monologue becomes slightly repetitive. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness. The piece successfully fulfills the prompt requirements while maintaining emotional authenticity and narrative coherence throughout. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 74.3
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The morning began, as all mornings did, with Midas trying to convince Chewy that they should eat the last of the tinned peaches. “They’re fermenting,” Midas said, holding the can up to the grey light filtering through the hole in the roof. “That’s basically vitamins. And alcohol. Which is also vitamins, if you think about it.” Chewy sat on the cracked linoleum, his fur a shade of grey that had once been white, his button eyes fixed on Midas with the patient, unblinking stare of a creature who had no stomach but an impeccable sense of moral superiority. A faint whirring came from somewhere inside his chest, like a very small, very tired fan. “Peaches are not a survival strategy,” Chewy said. His voice was a low, gravelly monotone, the result of a speaker that had been poorly re-soldered with a paperclip and a prayer. “We need protein. And a new roof. And, ideally, a reason to continue existing.” “We have a reason,” Midas said, popping the can open anyway. The smell was surprisingly pleasant. “We’re looking for the Sanctuary.” Chewy made a sound that might have been a sigh, if his vocal modulator had been capable of anything other than flat, mechanical disappointment. “The Sanctuary. The place you read about on a wall. In graffiti. Written by someone called ‘Fungi Dave.’ You trust Fungi Dave?” “I trust the penmanship,” Midas said, licking peach syrup off his thumb. “It was very confident. No shaky letters. That’s a man who knew where he was going.” The mission, such as it was, had a simple objective: follow Fungi Dave’s cryptic directions, scrawled in wobbly black paint on the side of a collapsed library, and locate the rumored refuge. The directions were: *“Past the weeping bridge, under the singing wire, where the big metal bird sleeps. Bring batteries. AAA.”* Chewy had pointed out that “weeping bridge” could mean a bridge that was structurally unsound, a bridge that had a sad story, or a bridge that was currently weeping water, which was the worst kind of bridge to cross. Midas had pointed out that Chewy was a stuffed animal who ran on spite and three double-A batteries, and that his opinion, while noted, was not binding. They set out at what Midas optimistically called “dawn,” which was actually about ten in the morning. The world was a patchwork of rust and weeds, where old roads had become rivers of brambles and the sky was the color of a forgotten teacup. Midas carried a backpack containing the peach can, a length of rope, and a map he’d drawn on a napkin. Chewy rode in the backpack’s front pouch, his head poking out, swiveling left and right like a furry periscope. “The singing wire,” Chewy said, as they approached a long-abandoned power line. The cables sagged between rusted pylons, and the wind played through them, producing a low, mournful hum. “That’s not singing. That’s a warning.” “It’s atmospheric,” Midas said. “It’s a C-sharp,” Chewy corrected. “And it’s flat.” They found the weeping bridge. It was, as predicted, weeping. A small stream had decided to use the bridge’s deck as its new bed, and the ancient timbers were slick with moss and algae. Midas stepped onto it, and the whole structure groaned like a dying whale. “This is a bad idea,” Chewy said. “That’s your default setting,” Midas replied, taking another step. The bridge did not collapse. It simply sagged a little more, depositing Midas waist-deep in the cold, brown water. Chewy, mercifully, stayed dry in the pouch. Midas swore, splashed, and emerged on the other side, dripping and smelling faintly of bog. “We’re making excellent progress,” Chewy said, with the dry satisfaction of a creature who had not gotten wet. The big metal bird was an old passenger jet, half-swallowed by the earth, its fuselage cracked open like a tin can. It lay in a clearing, surrounded by a forest of saplings that had grown through its wings. Midas approached it with a reverence he usually reserved for the last jar of pickles. “This is it,” he said. “The Sanctuary.” They found the entrance—a hole in the side of the plane, covered by a tarp. Midas pulled it back. Inside, it was dark, dry, and smelled of old carpet and mouse droppings. There was a lantern, a stack of canned beans, a sleeping bag, and a note. The note was written in the same confident hand as the graffiti. It read: *“Sorry. Got bored. Moved to the coast. Left you the beans. They’re from 2038. Should be fine. – Fungi Dave.”* Midas stared at the note for a long time. Chewy stared at Midas. “Well,” Chewy said. “That’s a win. We have beans.” “We walked six miles for beans,” Midas said, his voice hollow. “We walked six miles for *protein*,” Chewy corrected. “And a place to sleep that doesn’t have a hole in the roof. Sort of. The plane has a hole, but it’s a different hole. That’s progress.” They spent the night in the plane. Midas ate three cans of beans, one after the other, and felt a deep, philosophical regret. Chewy sat on the sleeping bag, his batteries running low, his voice growing fainter. “Tomorrow,” he said, his words crackling, “we find the coast.” “Why?” Midas asked, staring at the cracked ceiling. “Because Fungi Dave went there. And he had very good penmanship.” Midas laughed. It was a dry, wry sound, like paper crumpling. “You know, Chewy, for a koala with a dying speaker, you’re not half bad at this survival thing.” “I have no choice,” Chewy said, his button eyes glinting in the dim light. “You’d be dead in a week without me. Probably faster. You eat fermented peaches for breakfast.” “They were vitamins,” Midas said. “They were a cry for help.” The next morning, they left the plane, carrying a backpack full of beans and a new sense of purpose. The sky was still grey. The world was still rust and weeds. But somewhere, beyond the weeping bridges and the singing wires, there was a coast. And a man called Fungi Dave. And if nothing else, there would be batteries.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a solid understanding of Terry Pratchett's style, capturing his dry wit, philosophical observations wrapped in humor, and the ability to find absurdity in mundane situations. The author successfully creates a post-apocalyptic buddy comedy with genuine charm. The characterization is particularly strong. Midas emerges as a believably flawed protagonist - optimistic to a fault, prone to poor decisions, yet endearingly human in his determination to find meaning in a collapsed world. Chewy, the animatronic koala, serves as an excellent foil with his mechanical pessimism and sardonic observations. Their dynamic feels authentic and drives much of the story's humor. The dialogue sparkles with Pratchett-esque wit. Lines like "They were vitamins" / "They were a cry for help" capture the author's ability to blend humor with underlying pathos. The banter between the characters feels natural and serves both comedic and character development purposes. The mission structure works well, following the requested format of setup, inevitable failure, and return home "worse for wear." The quest for Fungi Dave's Sanctuary provides sufficient narrative drive while allowing for episodic mishaps. The bridge collapse and the ultimate anticlimax of finding only beans and a goodbye note effectively subvert expectations. However, the piece has some weaknesses. The world-building, while atmospheric, relies heavily on familiar post-apocalyptic imagery without much fresh detail. The "rust and weeds" description appears multiple times, suggesting limited descriptive vocabulary. Some moments veer toward telling rather than showing, particularly in character descriptions. The pacing occasionally drags, especially in the middle section with the bridge crossing. While the ending maintains the story's wry tone, it doesn't quite achieve the emotional complexity that elevates Pratchett's best work. The philosophical moments, while present, lack the deeper resonance of the master's writing. The prose is generally clean and readable, though it doesn't achieve true elegance. Some sentences feel workmanlike rather than artful, and there are missed opportunities for more vivid imagery or unexpected metaphors that would elevate the writing. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 13 Elegant Prose: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 12 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 8 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 9 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 7 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 5 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 15

+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 82.5
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The rain in Llanfyllin was a soft, persistent drizzle, the kind that seeped into wool and settled in the bones. Inside *Y Stori Fach*, the air was warm with the scent of old paper, beeswax, and brewing tea. Elin was perched on a stool behind the counter, a stack of faded Penguins waiting to be priced, when the bell above the door chimed a frantic, tinny note. A man burst in, shaking water from a dark coat. He was tall, with the kind of jawline that belonged on a coin, and hair the colour of wet sand that he pushed back from his forehead with a practiced, weary hand. He scanned the shop—the cluttered shelves, the sagging armchair by the window, the cat asleep on a pile of Welsh dictionaries—with the frantic relief of a fox who’d found a hole. “Thank God,” he breathed, his voice a rich, actorly baritone. “A sanctuary.” Elin did not look up from her price gun. “Bore da. Toilet’s through the back, if you need it. No charge for loitering, but we ask you buy a book if you plan to stay for the heat.” He laughed, a sound designed to disarm. “And here I thought the Welsh were famous for their hospitality.” He approached the counter, dripping a little puddle on the flagstones. “I’m hiding from the hounds. Four of them, with long lenses and no sense of personal space.” Elin finally raised her eyes. She saw the sharp cheekbones, the easy smile, the faint, expensive cologne that cut through the scent of damp wool. She also saw the desperation behind the charm. She knew that face. Everyone did. Rhys Caradog—star of that streaming epic about the Roman legions, the one who’d reportedly thrown a tantrum on set because a horse was prettier than him. “Ah,” she said, her voice flat. “The paparazzi. They’ve found our little corner of the world. How thrilling for us.” He tilted his head, a flicker of surprise in his blue eyes. He was used to a different reaction—a gasp, a stammer, an autograph request. “You’re not a fan, I take it.” “I’m a fan of dry floors. You’re dripping on my Caernarfon Castle jigsaws.” He looked down, genuinely chagrined. “I’m sorry. I’ll… I’ll buy something. To dry off.” He grabbed the nearest book from a display without looking. “This. Whatever it is.” Elin glanced at the spine. *The Sheep-Pig* by Dick King-Smith. “A fine choice. For a child’s birthday, I assume?” “For a man’s redemption,” he said, placing it on the counter with a theatrical flourish. He leaned in, lowering his voice. “Look. I know I’m ridiculous. I know I’m a walking cliché. But I’ve had a day. I was in Cardiff for a charity thing, and they tracked me. I just wanted to see the mountains. To breathe. And instead, I’m hiding in a bookshop from people who want a picture of me buying milk.” “You could have bought the milk. It’s a very good photo opportunity. ‘Star Supports Local Dairy.’” She rang up the book. “That’ll be six pounds fifty.” He stared at her, a slow smile spreading. It was less practiced now, more genuine. “You’re really not going to ask for a selfie, are you?” “I don’t take selfies. I take tea. Would you like one? You look like you’ve been chased by wolves, not photographers.” “I’d love one. I’m Rhys, by the way.” He offered his hand. She took it. Her palm was dry and cool; his was warm and slightly trembling. “Elin. And I know who you are. We have the internet here, even in Wales.” He laughed again, a real one this time, with a note of relief. “I’m so glad. I was worried I’d have to explain my entire filmography.” She poured the tea from a brown pot into two mismatched cups. “I’ve seen *The Eagle and the Raven*. You were good. The bit where you have to beg for your life—you did that well. It felt… real.” His face softened, the mask slipping for a second. “That scene was hard. I’ve never begged for my life, but I’ve begged for other things. For people to stay. For a part I didn’t get. For the audience to still love me after a flop.” Elin pushed the cup toward him. “And do they? Still love you?” “Mostly. But it’s a hungry love. It needs feeding. Every premiere, every interview, every perfectly timed joke. If I stop, they forget.” He wrapped his hands around the warm mug. “My father was a welder. He did a solid, honest job, and no one remembered his name the next day. I’ve spent my whole life terrified of being forgotten like that.” It was the first unscripted thing he’d said. Elin felt a small, unwelcome flutter in her chest. She busied herself with the price gun again. “That sounds exhausting. Performing for a world that’s always watching.” “It is.” He took a sip. “But you’re not watching. You’re pricing old books and shooing away famous actors from your jigsaws. It’s… disconcerting. And oddly thrilling.” “I’m not immune,” she said, before she could stop herself. The words came out clipped, defensive. “I just don’t know how to perform back. I’m better with books than people. They have dust jackets. It’s easier to know what you’re getting.” He set down his cup and looked at her with a directness that made her skin prickle. “What are you afraid of, Elin? If you don’t mind me asking. You’ve just heard my deepest fear—being forgotten. It’s only fair.” She hesitated. The rain tapped against the window. The cat stretched. “I’m afraid of the quiet,” she said, her voice low. “When the shop closes and I lock the door, and there’s no one here. Just the shelves. And the silence. It’s so loud, that silence. It tells me I’ve chosen a small life, and that no one is coming to save me from it.” The words hung between them, raw and unexpected. Rhys didn’t smile. He didn’t reach for her hand. He just nodded, slowly. “I know that silence,” he said. “It’s the same one that follows me after the curtain falls. When the applause dies and I’m alone in a hotel room in a city I don’t know.” Outside, a car door slammed. A voice shouted in English. “He went down this way!” Rhys tensed. Elin glanced at the door, then back at him. She grabbed a flat cap from a hook by the counter—her grandfather’s—and tossed it to him. “Put this on. Take the back door. It leads to the lane behind the chapel. You can cut through to the main road.” He pulled the cap low, a genuine smile breaking across his face. “You’re saving me.” “I’m saving my jigsaws from further water damage. Now go.” He paused at the back door, turning. “Thank you, Elin. For the tea. And for seeing me, not the performance.” “Don’t mention it,” she said. “And don’t forget your *Sheep-Pig*.” He laughed, soft and warm, and slipped out into the rain. The bell on the front door jangled as the paparazzi burst in, and Elin looked up, all business. “Can I help you, gentlemen? We have a lovely selection of local history. Or perhaps a jigsaw?”
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates a Welsh setting with authentic details (Llanfyllin, Y Stori Fach, Welsh phrases) and establishes the required dynamic between a charming but insecure actor and a seemingly immune bookstore owner. The characterization is particularly well-handled. Rhys Caradog is convincingly portrayed as someone whose charm is both genuine and performative, with his vulnerability emerging naturally through dialogue. His admission about begging for roles and fearing being forgotten feels authentic rather than forced. Elin's character is equally well-developed - her dry wit and apparent immunity to his charm masks a deeper loneliness that she reveals in a moment of unexpected honesty. The dialogue carries the scene effectively, with distinct voices for each character. Elin's clipped, practical responses ("Bore da. Toilet's through the back, if you need it") contrast sharply with Rhys's more theatrical speech patterns. The conversation flows naturally from surface-level banter to deeper emotional territory without feeling rushed or unearned. The prose strikes a good balance between atmospheric description and dialogue-driven action. The opening paragraph establishes mood and setting efficiently, while descriptive passages like "hair the colour of wet sand" and "the frantic relief of a fox who'd found a hole" add color without becoming overwrought. The emotional complexity is handled well - both characters reveal vulnerabilities that mirror each other (fear of being forgotten vs. fear of silence/smallness), creating genuine connection. The romantic tension builds subtly through their verbal sparring and moments of recognition. However, there are some minor weaknesses. Certain phrases veer toward the overly literary ("actorly baritone," "with the kind of jawline that belonged on a coin"), and the ending, while satisfying, resolves perhaps too neatly with Elin's rescue of Rhys. The piece also relies somewhat heavily on familiar romance tropes, though it executes them competently. The scene maintains coherence throughout and avoids major structural problems. The pacing works well, building from initial antagonism through mutual recognition to a moment of genuine connection and practical resolution. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 16 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16

+ Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 80.7
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The foghorn’s first moan rolled through the salt-crusted stones of the underhold, a dirge swallowed by the oppressive silence that had settled like a shroud. Aya’s hand trembled against the iron door, the cold biting through her glove. Behind her, Katra’s breath came in short, sharp hisses, while Simeon’s weight shifted uneasily on the creaking floorboards. The corridor beyond the entrance—a narrow throat of brick and mortar, lit by the guttering flame of a single gas jet—felt wrong. Too still. Too clean. The chaos of the raid above had been a symphony of splintering wood and shouted curses; this was the quiet before a hangman’s knot tightens. “It’s a trap,” Simeon muttered, his voice a gravelly rasp. He clutched a rusted prybar—his preferred tool, for all the good it would do. “I’ve walked into fewer ambushes than this smells of.” “You’ve walked into *any* ambush smelling of gin and bad decisions,” Katra snapped, her fingers twitching toward the small pistol at her hip. She was all sharp angles and sharper temper, a woman carved from dockside desperation. “But he’s not wrong, Aya. This ain’t right.” Aya forced her jaw to unclench. The immateria hummed at the edges of her awareness, a dark, oily presence that coiled beneath her skin like a second pulse. Her mother’s gift. Her mother’s curse. *Hanged by the neck until dead.* The memory was a cold stone in her gut. She pushed it down. “The artifact is below. If we turn back now, we lose the guild to the first rat who crawls out of the woodwork. We go forward.” She turned the handle. The door swung inward with a groan of iron hinges, and the underhold opened before them like a tomb. It was a vaulted chamber, carved from the bedrock beneath the guild’s main hall. Water dripped somewhere in the dark, a metronomic *plink, plink* against stone. The air was thick with the smell of damp wool, old blood, and the acrid tang of oiled machinery. Gas lamps hissed along the walls, casting pools of jaundiced light across a floor littered with crates and rusted chains. At the chamber’s heart, a pedestal of black marble stood empty—the scythan artifact, a jagged shard of obsidian that hummed with a forgotten power, was conspicuously absent. And seated upon a high-backed chair of oak and whalebone, as if she had been waiting for tea to be served, was Guild Master Viktoria Krasna. Aya’s blood turned to ice. Viktoria was a woman of fifty winters, with a face like a granite cliff and hair the color of iron filings, pulled back in a severe bun. She wore a coat of deep blue wool, brass buttons gleaming, and her hands rested on the head of a blackthorn cane. At her sides stood two figures—hulking brutes in leather aprons, their faces obscured by brass goggles and scarves. One held a lantern that burned with a sickly green flame; the other cradled a short, wide-muzzled shotgun. “Miss Aya,” Viktoria said, her voice a low, honeyed rasp. “Or should I say, the ‘Shade of the Docks’? I confess, I had my suspicions. The way you moved. The way you spoke. Too refined for a gutter rat. But I needed proof.” She gestured with the cane, and the brute with the lantern stepped forward, casting the green light across Aya’s face. “And here you are. Delivered.” Aya’s throat tightened. *No. No, this cannot be.* She had been so careful. The mask, the voice, the borrowed clothes. But Viktoria was no fool. “You were never arrested,” Aya said, the words scraping out of her. “The raid—it was a feint.” “A feint?” Viktoria’s lips curled into a smile that did not reach her eyes. “My dear, it was a *trap*. I knew the constabulary would come sniffing after the salt-smuggling operation. I simply let them have a few sacrificial lambs—and a decoy that looked remarkably like me—while I waited here for the rats to scurry for the cheese.” She tapped the cane against the floor. “And here you are. All three of you.” Katra drew her pistol with a snarl, but the shotgun’s roar was faster. The bullet caught her in the shoulder, spinning her to the ground with a cry of pain. Simeon lunged forward with the prybar, but the second brute caught him by the throat and slammed him against the wall, the iron bar clattering to the stone. Aya raised her hands, the immateria surging, dark tendrils of shadow writhing at her fingertips—but Viktoria was already moving, the cane swinging in a wide arc. The blackthorn connected with Aya’s wrist, and a jolt of cold, numbing pain shot up her arm. The shadows dissipated like smoke in a gale. “None of that,” Viktoria said, stepping closer. The brute with the lantern seized Aya by the hair, yanking her head back. “Your mother tried the same trick. It didn’t save her neck, either.” Aya’s mask—a simple leather half-face—was torn away, the ties snapping. The cold air hit her cheeks, and she felt naked, exposed in a way that went beyond the physical. *They will know. They will tell my father. The aristocracy will cast me out. I will hang, like Mother.* Her breath came in ragged gasps as she was forced to her knees, the stone floor cold and damp through her trousers. Viktoria circled her, the cane tapping a slow rhythm. “A noblewoman. A *Lady* Aya, of the House Morska-Volkov. Dabbling in gutter crime and forbidden magicka. Your father will be *thrilled* to hear of this. The scandal alone will ruin him.” Aya’s mind raced, a frantic scramble for purchase. *Stall. Bargain. Survive.* “You don’t want to kill me,” she said, her voice steadier than she felt. “I’m worth more alive. My family’s name—my connections—I can open doors for the guild that no amount of smuggled salt ever could.” Viktoria stopped, tilting her head. “Is that so?” “The constabulary, the harbor masters, the trade commissioners—they all owe my father favors. I can leverage that. I can make the guild untouchable.” Aya’s hands were shaking, but she forced herself to meet Viktoria’s gaze. “Kill me, and you lose that. Keep me as a hostage—a bargaining chip—and you gain everything.” The foghorn sounded again, a mournful bellow that echoed through the underhold. Viktoria was silent for a long moment, her eyes searching Aya’s face. Then she laughed—a low, dry sound like stones grinding together. “You have your mother’s tongue,” she said. “And her audacity. Very well, Lady Aya. I will keep you alive. For now.” She gestured to the brutes. “Bind them. The girl goes to the water cell. The man goes to the steam room. And the *Lady*”—she smiled, a predator’s smile—“she comes with me. We have much to discuss.” Aya’s heart hammered as the brutes closed in, the cold iron of manacles biting into her wrists. *I am alive. For now.* The thought was no comfort. The immateria whispered in the dark of her mind, a promise and a threat. But as she was dragged into the deeper shadows of the underhold, the foghorn’s third moan rolled through the night, and Aya wondered if her mother had heard such a sound before the trapdoor opened beneath her feet.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and atmospheric worldbuilding within the whalepunk fantasy setting. The author successfully creates a Victorian-gothic atmosphere through careful attention to sensory details - the foghorn's mournful calls, the smell of salt and oil, the hissing gas lamps, and the oppressive underground setting all contribute to an immersive experience. The dialogue feels authentic to the established world, with characters speaking in distinct voices that reflect their backgrounds. Viktoria's refined menace contrasts effectively with Katra's street-smart vernacular and Simeon's gruff practicality. Aya's internal voice captures both her aristocratic education and her growing desperation as her carefully constructed double life unravels. Character motivations remain consistent throughout. Viktoria's revelation as the orchestrator rather than victim of the raid feels earned given her established cunning, and Aya's attempt to negotiate using her aristocratic connections demonstrates both intelligence and desperation. The piece effectively shows rather than tells emotional states through physical reactions and dialogue. However, the writing occasionally veers toward purple prose, particularly in atmospheric descriptions. Phrases like "a dirge swallowed by the oppressive silence that had settled like a shroud" and "the quiet before a hangman's knot tightens" feel somewhat overwrought. The repeated foghorn motif, while atmospheric, borders on heavy-handed symbolism. The pacing works well for a confrontation scene, building tension through the too-quiet approach before revealing the trap. The author successfully avoids writing a fight scene as requested, instead focusing on psychological tension and power dynamics. Aya's unmasking carries appropriate weight both literally and metaphorically. The ending maintains the dark tone established throughout, with Aya's temporary reprieve feeling precarious rather than triumphant. The reference to her mother's execution provides effective emotional resonance and foreshadowing. Some minor weaknesses include occasional redundancy in description and a few moments where the prose becomes slightly convoluted. The piece also relies somewhat heavily on familiar genre tropes, though it executes them competently. + Fanfic-crossover: Gunpowder Rendezvous — Score: 75.5
Prompt:
Gunpowder Rendezvous Spike Spiegel & Revy Write a story where Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop) and Revy (Black Lagoon) meet in a seedy bar after both being hired for the same bounty. Their mutual attraction is complicated by their trust issues and competitive instincts. Include drinking, gunplay, and unresolved sexual tension that culminates in a motel room standoff. **Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop)** Spike is a laid-back bounty hunter with a dark past as a syndicate enforcer. He practices Jeet Kune Do and is exceptionally skilled with firearms. He's chronically broke, constantly hungry, and addicted to cigarettes. Despite his carefree attitude, he's haunted by his past and the woman he left behind. He has a prosthetic eye from a past injury that affects his depth perception. He's fatalistic to the extreme, often saying he's already died once. He values freedom above all else and dislikes attachments, though he secretly cares deeply for his crew. Famous quote: "Whatever happens, happens." **Revy/Rebecca Lee (Black Lagoon)** Revy is a Chinese-American dual-wielding gunslinger with unparalleled shooting skills. She's foul-mouthed, chain-smoking, and has severe anger management issues stemming from childhood abuse. She's known as "Two Hands" for her ability to accurately fire two pistols simultaneously. She drinks heavily and solves most problems with violence. Despite her hardened exterior, she has moments of vulnerability, particularly around Rock. She has a dragon tattoo covering her right arm and typically wears extremely short shorts and a black crop top regardless of the situation. Famous quote: "I'm not a good person, not a bad person. I'm just here." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The neon jukebox hiccups, then grinds through another loop of the same scratchy rock track. The bartender, a man with a face like a clenched fist, doesn’t bother changing it. No one complains. The clientele of this bar don’t complain about anything louder than a gunshot. Spike Spiegel sits at the far end of the counter, one elbow in a puddle of cheap whiskey. He watches the door through the smoke of his cigarette. The bounty is a fat man named Chen, last seen three hours ago with a briefcase full of red mercury. Spike’s information is good. He’s early. The door swings open. A woman walks in like she owns the place and everyone in it owes her rent. She’s wearing a black crop top and shorts so short they’re almost a rumor. A dragon coils up her right arm, scales glistening with sweat. She has two pistols holstered at her hips, the grips worn smooth. She scans the room with the flat, predatory gaze of someone who has never had to look away from a fight. She sits down two stools away from Spike. “Whiskey,” she says. Her voice is a rasp, scraped clean of anything soft. The bartender pours. She downs it in one swallow, then lights a cigarette. Her eyes flick to Spike. “You’re in my spot.” Spike grins, lazy and unbothered. “Lot of spots in a bar this empty.” “There’s a fat man with a briefcase,” she says. “He’s mine.” Spike’s grin doesn’t waver. “Funny. I was just thinking the same thing.” The jukebox skips. The guitar riff repeats. Neither of them breaks eye contact. “Revy,” she says, as if the name should mean something. “Spike.” She snorts. “What kind of name is that for a man?” “The kind that’s been dead once already,” he says. He stubs out his cigarette. “You want to settle this the hard way?” Revy’s hand drifts toward her holster. “I only know one way.” The bartender disappears into the back room. Smart man. Spike stands slowly, hands loose at his sides. He’s all wire and languid motion, like a cat that’s been cornered but hasn’t decided to care yet. “You’re good with those,” he says, nodding at her pistols. “I can tell by the way you carry them.” “I’m better than good.” “So am I.” The tension stretches thin as a wire. The jukebox finishes the song. For a moment, there is absolute silence. Then Revy laughs. It’s a harsh, ugly sound, but there’s something real in it. “You’re crazy.” “So I’ve been told.” She slides a bottle of whiskey off the counter and takes a long pull. She holds it out to him. He takes it. Their fingers brush. Neither pulls away. “One shot,” she says. “Then we find Chen. Whoever gets him first keeps the score.” “And the loser?” Revy’s eyes glitter. “The loser buys the next round.” They find Chen in a warehouse by the docks. He’s trying to bribe a local crew into smuggling him out. The negotiation is going poorly. It gets worse when two people kick in the door from opposite sides. Spike moves like water, flowing around crates and debris. His pistol is an extension of his arm. He takes out two guards before they can raise their weapons. Revy is a storm. She fires both pistols simultaneously, the shots overlapping into a single thunderclap. Two more men drop. She doesn’t stop moving, doesn’t stop shooting. Chen scrambles for his briefcase. Revy kicks it out of his hand. Spike catches it one-handed, mid-air. They stand on either side of the fat man, guns trained on him, breathing hard. “That was mine,” Revy says. “You hesitated,” Spike replies. “Half a second. You wanted to see what I’d do.” She doesn’t deny it. Her lips curl into something that isn’t quite a smile. “Maybe I did.” Chen whimpers. They both ignore him. “Split it,” Spike says. “Or we keep dancing.” Revy holsters one gun. She holds out her hand. “Half.” He tosses her the briefcase. She catches it, tucks it under her arm. For a long moment, they just look at each other. The warehouse is dark and quiet, smelling of cordite and salt. “There’s a motel down the street,” she says. “Cheap. Dirty.” “Sounds perfect.” The motel room is exactly as advertised. The carpet is stained. The air conditioner rattles like it’s dying. The bedspread has seen things no fabric should remember. They don’t turn on the light. The briefcase sits on the dresser, unopened. They stand on opposite sides of the room, guns still holstered, but hands close. “I don’t trust you,” Revy says. “Good,” Spike replies. “I don’t trust anyone.” She steps forward. He meets her halfway. Their mouths collide, hungry and violent, more fight than kiss. Her hands fist in his jacket. His fingers dig into her hip. They stumble against the wall, and the cheap plaster cracks. She breaks away, breathing hard. Her eyes are wild. “This doesn’t mean anything.” “Whatever happens, happens.” She laughs again, that same ugly, real sound. She draws her pistol and presses the barrel against his chest. He doesn’t flinch. He draws his own gun and presses it to her stomach. They stand there, locked in a standoff, hearts hammering against the triggers. “First one to blink loses,” she says. Spike smiles, soft and tired. “I’ve already lost everything that matters.” Revy’s finger rests on the trigger. Her eyes search his face for something—a lie, a crack, a reason to pull. She doesn’t find one. Slowly, she lowers her gun. He does the same. The neon sign outside flickers red through the blinds. The jukebox in the bar down the street starts playing again, faint and scratchy. Revy holsters her pistol. She picks up the briefcase and tosses it onto the bed. “Your half’s in there,” she says. “Take it and go.” Spiegel lights a cigarette. He doesn’t move toward the door. “I’m not going anywhere,” he says. She watches him through the smoke. For once, she has nothing to say. The motel room settles into a strange, fragile peace. Two killers, a briefcase of red mercury, and the ghost of something that might, under different stars, have been a beginning.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and character understanding while delivering on the prompt's specific requirements. The author captures both Spike and Revy's essential characteristics effectively - Spike's fatalistic cool and Revy's volatile aggression are well-rendered through both action and dialogue. The story structure follows a clear arc from initial confrontation to grudging respect to sexual tension, culminating in the requested motel standoff. The prose style is lean and atmospheric, with effective noir sensibilities that suit both source materials. Descriptions like "She walks in like she owns the place and everyone in it owes her rent" and "breathing hard" capture character essence efficiently. The dialogue feels authentic to both characters, particularly Revy's blunt directness and Spike's philosophical detachment. The action sequences are well-choreographed and distinct for each character - Spike flowing "like water" while Revy is "a storm." This differentiation shows understanding of their respective fighting styles. The sexual tension builds naturally through their competitive dynamic rather than feeling forced. However, the piece has some weaknesses. The ending feels somewhat abrupt and the resolution of their standoff lacks the emotional weight it could have carried. While the characters are recognizable, they don't quite achieve the depth of nuance that would elevate this from competent to exceptional. Some descriptions border on cliché ("eyes glitter," "storm" metaphors) though they remain functional. The story succeeds in its primary goals but doesn't transcend them. It's a solid execution of the prompt that captures the essence of both characters and delivers the requested elements without significant innovation or emotional depth that would make it memorable beyond its competent craft. + Folk horror: Útiseta — Score: 82.5
Prompt:
Folk Horror: Útiseta Write a chapter set in rural northern Sweden in the mid-1990s. Linnea, a teenage girl from Stockholm, has reluctantly moved to a small village with her parents after her father's job transfer. She's befriended Astrid, a local girl who claims her grandmother taught her ancient practices passed down through generations. Astrid has been introducing Linnea to "midnight games" that involve old rituals, convincing her they can use them to learn classmates' secrets or influence their behavior. Tonight, Astrid has convinced Linnea to participate in Útiseta—sitting out on an ancient burial mound at midnight to "receive knowledge from beyond." The chapter focuses on this single midnight excursion, the ritualistic preparations, Astrid's matter-of-fact instructions that mix teenage rebellion with eerily specific traditional elements, and the mounting tension as Linnea becomes increasingly uncertain whether they're simply scaring themselves or if something is actually responding to their presence. The narrative should maintain ambiguity about whether the strange occurrences are supernatural or psychological, while establishing the dangerous line they're crossing by treating ancient practices as entertainment. First person, past tense, told as Linnea's recollection of events in the present day (though this device should be largely transparent). 1000 words. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The church bell chimed once. Not the full, ponderous twelve I’d been bracing for, counting the seconds in the dark. Just a single, clear note that hung in the frozen air like a silver thread, then dissolved into the absolute silence of the forest. It was midnight. I had checked my watch. It was not a Sunday. There was no reason for the bell to ring. Beside me, Astrid did not flinch. She sat cross-legged on the packed earth of the burial mound, her back straight, her hands resting on her knees, palms up. In the faint wash of starlight—there was no moon—she looked less like a girl and more like a carved figure from a stave church, something old and patient and not quite human. “He’s here,” she whispered. A trickle of cold sweat ran down my ribs. *He.* Not *they.* Not *the spirits.* The specificity of it was worse than any ghost story. I had been in the village for three months. Stockholm felt like a fever dream—neon lights, the hiss of the tunnelbana, the reassuring anonymity of a thousand faces. Here, the faces were few, and they all knew I was the outsider, the city girl with the wrong accent and the wrong shoes. Astrid was the only one who had spoken to me without that look of pitying curiosity. She had been a revelation. She knew the names of every moss and lichen, could read the weather in the way the birch leaves turned, and she spoke of her grandmother’s teachings with the same casual authority other girls used to discuss pop stars. The midnight games had started small: leaving milk in saucers for the *tomte*, tying red ribbons around a rowan tree to bind a crush’s affection. Harmless. A thrill. But tonight was different. Útiseta. Sitting out. “You have to empty your mind,” Astrid had instructed me as we walked through the forest, her flashlight beam cutting a narrow, trembling path through the pines. “The mound is a threshold. The dead are close to the surface here. They can hear you. They can *answer* you, if you ask the right way.” She had made me wash my face in a stream so cold it felt like a burn. She had tied a strip of linen around my wrist, woven with three knots. “For protection,” she’d said, but her voice had an edge I hadn’t heard before. “Don’t undo them until we’re back inside your house.” The mound was not a dramatic thing. It was a low, grassy hump in a clearing, ringed by ancient, gnarled pines whose roots clutched the earth like arthritic fingers. In daylight, it was just a curiosity. At midnight, it was a presence. The air was colder here, denser, as if it had been breathed in and out by something vast and sleeping. We had sat in silence for what felt like an hour. The forest had gone still. No owl. No rustle of a fox. Even the wind had dropped, leaving us in a bubble of absolute quiet. I had tried to empty my mind, as Astrid had said, but my thoughts were a swarm of gnats: my father’s forced cheer at dinner, the taunts of the boys at school, the terrible, gnawing loneliness that had become my constant companion. Then the bell chimed. And Astrid said, *He’s here.* I wanted to run. My legs were rigid, locked in place. I could feel the cold of the earth seeping up through my jeans, the damp of the moss against my palms. The linen band on my wrist felt tight, like a hand gripping me. “Don’t look,” Astrid hissed. “Don’t turn your head. If you see him, you’ll be bound to him. Just listen.” I listened. There was nothing. The silence was so complete it was a noise of its own, a high, thin ringing in my ears. I stared at the black wall of pines, at the spaces between the trunks where the darkness seemed to pulse, to breathe. I felt a pressure in the air, a change in the barometric weight of the night. The hair on my arms stood up. A twig snapped. Close. Too close. My breath caught. I could smell something now—damp wool, turned earth, and beneath it, a faint, sweet rot, like the core of an apple left too long in a cellar. *Ask,* said a voice that was not a voice. It was not in my ears. It was in my skull, a thought that had not been born of my own mind. *Ask, and I will show you.* I thought of the boy who had mocked my Stockholm accent. I thought of the girl who had poured juice on my homework. I could know their secrets. I could make them feel small. The pressure increased. The smell grew stronger. The darkness between the pines seemed to solidify, to take on a shape that was not quite human, but was trying to be. “Astrid,” I whispered, my voice cracking. “I want to go.” “One more minute,” she breathed. “He’s close. I can feel him. Just one more.” But I could not stay. Something in me—the part that still remembered streetlights and bus schedules—screamed that this was not a game. This was a door, and we were standing in its frame, and the thing on the other side was not interested in teenage pranks. I stood up. My legs shook. I did not look at the shape in the trees. I undid the first knot on my wrist. Astrid gasped. “Don’t!” I undid the second. The pressure vanished. The smell of rot was gone, replaced by clean, cold pine. A normal owl called from somewhere far off. I undid the third knot and threw the linen strip onto the mound. I grabbed Astrid’s arm and pulled her to her feet. She was crying, I realized. Silent tears streaming down her face. We ran. We did not stop until we reached the edge of the village, until the yellow light from her kitchen window cut through the dark like a blade. I never asked her what she saw. I never asked her what she heard. But I know what I heard. I know the church bell did not ring. I checked the next morning. The clockwork was broken. It had been silent for six months. And I know that when I closed my eyes that night, I saw a shape in the pines, and it was smiling.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and atmospheric control while successfully fulfilling most of the prompt requirements. The author establishes an effective folk horror atmosphere through careful pacing, sensory details, and mounting tension. The rural Swedish setting feels authentic, with specific cultural references like the tunnelbana and tomte that ground the narrative in place. The first-person retrospective narration works well, maintaining the requested transparency while allowing for mature reflection on teenage experiences. Linnea emerges as a believable protagonist - her isolation as a Stockholm transplant, her desperation for friendship, and her growing unease feel psychologically authentic. The progression from harmless "midnight games" to genuine terror is well-calibrated. Astrid functions effectively as both friend and potential antagonist, though she remains somewhat underdeveloped beyond her role as conduit to the supernatural. Her matter-of-fact delivery of occult instructions creates the requested blend of teenage rebellion and ancient tradition. The prose demonstrates strong technical control with evocative imagery ("darkness seemed to pulse, to breathe") and effective sensory details (the smell of "damp wool, turned earth, and beneath it, a faint, sweet rot"). The pacing builds tension methodically, and the ambiguity between psychological and supernatural explanations is maintained throughout. However, some elements feel slightly overwrought - phrases like "silver thread" and "carved figure from a stave church" occasionally tip toward purple prose. The ending, while atmospheric, relies on familiar horror tropes (the broken bell, the smiling shape) that don't quite transcend genre conventions. The piece succeeds in creating genuine unease and maintains narrative coherence throughout. The cultural details feel researched rather than appropriated, and the folk horror elements integrate naturally with the coming-of-age framework. The writing demonstrates sophistication in its restraint - knowing when to show and when to withhold information. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Physical-spatial: Therapeutic Boundaries — Score: 89.1
Prompt:
**"Therapeutic Boundaries"** Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a former professional athlete undergoing mandatory physical therapy after a career-destroying injury, harboring resentment toward recovery itself. Their physiotherapist is not only unnervingly attractive but also a former training partner from their competitive days who has successfully transitioned to a new career while the protagonist's identity remains shattered. We'll lean into the sexual tension of the scene by having her be attracted to him, but both of them attempting to maintain mental distance while being in physical contact as he shows her stretches & movements & manipulates her body, navigating the thresholds of pain. This scene MUST maintain complete physical coherence—every movement, stretch, and manipulation must be anatomically precise and spatially consistent. Paint an exceptionally vivid sensory picture through detailed attention to pain thresholds, involuntary responses to touch. Capture the suppressed, flaring tension of physical proximity, touch, and the controlled infliction of uncomfortable movements. Physical clarity is essential — readers must understand exactly what is happening with the body during each therapeutic technique. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The diagram looms. A flayed man, rendered in clinical pinks and crimsons, his muscles teased apart like the threads of a frayed rope. I know that map better than I know my own face now. The rectus femoris, the vastus medialis, the adductor magnus—these are the names of my betrayers. The treatment table is cold under my back. The paper gown crinkles with every breath, a cheap, sterile whisper against my skin. I am a collection of parts that no longer assemble into a whole. “Hip flexor’s still tight,” he says. His voice hasn’t changed. That low, even tone he used to use in the final meters of a race, a calm that was more terrifying than any shout. I don’t answer. I stare at the ceiling tiles, counting the tiny perforations. He is Marcus. Former training partner. Now, my keeper. He stands at the foot of the table, a silhouette against the harsh fluorescent light. He’s filled out, but not soft. His shoulders have broadened, the neck thicker. The body that once moved in perfect, explosive synchrony with mine now moves with a deliberate, clinical precision. He wears a simple black polo shirt, the sleeves snug around his biceps. He doesn’t have to try. That’s what galls me. The ease of his transition. He traded the track for a degree, the podium for a white coat. He rebuilt himself. I am a ruin. “I need to get into the psoas,” he says, and the word hangs in the air between us, intimate and invasive. “It’s deep. You’re going to feel this.” I already feel everything. The ghost of the impact. The phantom sprint of a hamstring that will never fire again. The grinding, low-grade ache of a patella that was shattered into a dozen pieces and screwed back together like a child’s broken toy. He moves to my side. The wheels of his stool whisper on the linoleum. He sits, and suddenly he is close. I can smell him. Clean soap, a hint of antiseptic, and underneath it, the familiar, sharp scent of his skin. A scent I used to inhale on the starting line, a scent that meant competition, adrenaline, the imminent explosion of effort. Now it means this. His hands are warm. He finds the bony landmark of my anterior superior iliac spine with his thumb, a touch that is impersonal and devastatingly precise. His other hand slides under my lower back, fingers splaying against the erector spinae. The contact is a shock. A current. I tense, a full-body clench that travels from my jaw to my toes. “Breathe,” he says. Not a request. An instruction. I hate him for that. For the calm. For the competence. For the way his thumb presses, slowly, deliberately, into the belly of the muscle, finding the knot of scar tissue that feels like a stone buried in my flesh. “There it is,” he murmurs, more to himself than to me. The pressure increases. It’s not sharp. It’s a deep, nauseating ache, a sense of wrongness being prodded. My hand shoots out, grabbing the edge of the table. The metal is cold and unforgiving. “Let it go,” he says. His face is inches from mine. I can see the faint stubble on his jaw, the way his brow is furrowed in concentration. He is not looking at me. He is looking at the map on the wall, translating it to the territory under his hands. “I can’t,” I grind out. The words are a confession. He shifts his weight. His knee brushes against my hip. A casual, necessary adjustment of his own anatomy, but it sends a jolt through me. The heat of his leg through the thin fabric of his trousers. The proximity. The sheer, unbearable intimacy of this mechanical violation. “You can,” he says. “The muscle is guarding. It thinks it’s still protecting the joint. You have to tell it the fight is over.” The fight is over. The words are a knife. They pierce the professional membrane of the moment. He knows. He knows the fight is all I had. He was there. He saw the last race. He saw the way my leg buckled, the way my knee went sideways, the sound I made that wasn’t a scream but a sob of pure, physical betrayal. His thumb moves. A slow, grinding circle into the psoas. The pain blooms, hot and deep, radiating down into my groin, up into my ribs. I gasp. My back arches involuntarily, pressing my spine harder against his other hand. “Good,” he says. “That’s the release.” It feels like destruction. My eyes are wet. I blink furiously, staring at the diagram. The psoas is a deep, hidden thing, a muscle of the soul, they say. It connects the spine to the leg. It is the muscle of the runner’s stride, the dancer’s arabesque. It is the muscle of flight. He is holding it. Holding the core of what I was. His fingers are strong, unyielding. I think about how those hands used to grip mine in a post-race handshake, firm and quick. Now they are inside me, navigating the wreckage. He eases the pressure, just a fraction. The relief is a wave, almost erotic in its intensity. My breath shudders out of me. “Better,” he says. He doesn’t remove his hand. He holds the position, letting the muscle settle. His thumb is a point of heat against the deep, cold ache. I turn my head. Our eyes meet. For a second, the clinical distance collapses. I see him. Not the physiotherapist. The man I used to chase. The man who beat me by a hair at Nationals, the man whose post-race grin was a mirror of my own. He sees me. The ghost. The ruin. “Your form is good,” he says, his voice softer. “You’re holding a lot of tension in your jaw.” I don’t answer. I can’t. The words are lodged in the same place as the pain. He holds my gaze for a beat longer than is necessary. Then he looks back down at his hands, at the body on the table. He begins to guide my leg into a new stretch, a gentle, external rotation. His hand cups my knee, his other hand supporting my ankle. The movement is slow, controlled. The joint protests, a whisper of the old agony. “Just to the edge,” he says. I let him take me there. To the threshold. To the place where the pain is sharp and bright, a line I am terrified to cross. He holds me right on the edge, his fingers a steady, commanding presence. The tension between us is a live wire, humming with everything unsaid. The resentment. The attraction. The shared history that makes this clinical touch feel like a violation and a benediction all at once. The flayed man on the wall watches, a silent god of sinew and bone. I am his subject. And Marcus is the priest, holding me at the precipice of my own broken body, waiting for me to surrender.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional technical skill and emotional depth in executing a challenging prompt. The narrative successfully maintains the required first-person perspective of a former athlete undergoing physical therapy, with the complex dynamic between protagonist and therapist expertly rendered. The anatomical precision is outstanding - the writer demonstrates clear knowledge of muscle groups (psoas, rectus femoris, vastus medialis, adductor magnus) and therapeutic techniques. Each physical manipulation is spatially coherent and medically accurate, from the hip flexor work to the psoas release to the external rotation stretch. The progression of movements follows logical therapeutic sequence. The sensory writing is vivid and immersive. Pain is rendered with specificity - "deep, nauseating ache," "grinding, low-grade ache," "hot and deep, radiating down into my groin, up into my ribs." The physical responses feel authentic: the involuntary tensing, the gasping, the arched back. The sexual tension is palpable but appropriately restrained, conveyed through proximity, scent, and the charged nature of necessary touch. Character development is nuanced. The protagonist's resentment toward recovery, shattered identity, and complex feelings toward Marcus are convincingly portrayed. Marcus emerges as competent yet not unsympathetic, with hints of his own awareness of the charged situation. The shared history adds meaningful depth. The prose is sophisticated without being overwrought. Metaphors like "I am a collection of parts that no longer assemble into a whole" and "The flayed man on the wall watches, a silent god of sinew and bone" are striking but earned. The voice remains consistent throughout - bitter, vulnerable, yet retaining traces of athletic precision in observation. The piece maintains excellent pacing, building tension through the physical therapy session while revealing character and backstory organically. The ending appropriately leaves the protagonist at a threshold - both physical and emotional - without forcing resolution. Minor weaknesses include occasional repetition of certain images and perhaps slightly heavy-handed symbolism with the anatomical diagram, though these don't significantly detract from the overall achievement. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 2 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
